summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's decision to terminate Epic Games Sweden's developer account, which would have allowed Epic to launch an iOS game store in the EU under the Digital Markets Act. He finds Epic's argument that the DMA forbids Apple from any oversight over marketplace developers unconvincing, but also criticizes Apple's reasoning — particularly citing Sweeney's critical tweets as justification rather than sticking to Epic's 2020 contractual breach. Gruber argues Apple should have either made a clean 'lifetime ban' argument based on Epic's Fortnite payment stunt or taken the opportunity to look magnanimous. He notes that Apple citing a U.S. court ruling to ban a Swedish subsidiary raises interesting jurisdictional questions under EU law.
key_points: Epic's argument that the DMA forbids Apple from having any discretion over who runs app marketplaces doesn't hold up to common sense — there must be some line a developer can cross
Apple weakened its own position by citing Sweeney's critical tweets as grounds for termination, making it look like Apple punishes developers for speaking out rather than for rule-breaking
The distinction between Spotify and Epic isn't rhetoric — both are vociferous App Store critics, but only Epic deliberately broke contractual rules
Apple citing a U.S. court decision to ban Epic's Swedish subsidiary creates a fascinating jurisdictional question since Epic never lost a lawsuit to Apple in the EU
Gruber believes Apple missed a PR opportunity to look magnanimous and instead went out of its way to look vindictive
money_quotes: There's got to be some sort of line a developer can cross that would justify Apple revoking their developer account.
If Apple wants to make that a 'lifetime' ban, they should just say so.

Citing recent tweets that are simply critical — even scathingly critical — just makes it look like Apple's policy is that if a developer criticizes the App Store's rules, Apple will punish them for speaking out.
The difference between Spotify and Epic isn't in their rhetoric; it's in their past behavior.
Stick to the fact that Epic deliberately broke the rules with the Fortnite payments stunt. Ignore the tweets arguing that Apple is abusing a monopoly.
But why not take an opportunity to look magnanimous? Apple shouldn't be expected to grovel, but this looks like they're going out of their way to look vindictive.
I really thought it would be a clever bit of public relations jujitsu to make nice with Epic, even if, in Cupertino, it was through gritted teeth.
Popcorn-eating-wise, I'm genuinely curious about Apple citing a U.S. court decision as grounds for banning Epic's Swedish subsidiary from holding a developer account.
key_insight: Apple had a strong case based on Epic's 2020 contractual breach but undermined it by mixing in objections to Sweeney's public criticism, making Apple look vindictive rather than principled at a moment when magnanimity would have been strategically smarter.
